subject: Rheumatology
title: Mixed connective tissue disease
preambleMarkdown:
# Mixed connective tissue disease



> **Clinical-review notice:** This is a bullet-first examination and clinical-orientation draft. Local protocols, medication doses, procedural thresholds, pregnancy/pediatric pathways, emergency workflows, and jurisdiction-specific guidance must be checked before clinical use.

sections.heading: Overview
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Mixed connective tissue disease is a clinical condition or syndrome in rheumatology.
- This article focuses on inflammation, autoimmunity, joints, systemic features, serology, imaging, and immunosuppression.
- The practical clinical priority is rheumatology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Start by deciding whether the presentation is stable, time-sensitive, complicated, recurrent, or incidental.
- Imported source notes are preserved below for clinical review and citation reconciliation.

sections.heading: Key Points
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify unstable, time-sensitive, or function-threatening presentations first.
- Anchor the assessment of Mixed connective tissue disease to the patient context: age, pregnancy status when relevant, immune status, comorbid disease, medications, prior episodes, and local resources.
- Use focused history and examination before broad testing.
- Choose investigations that change diagnosis, severity assessment, treatment, disposition, or follow-up.
- Treat reversible danger early and give clear safety-net instructions.

sections.heading: Definition
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Mixed connective tissue disease is best defined by the specific clinical problem, anatomic site, mechanism, severity, and care setting.
- For this topic, the core concept is rheumatology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Separate suspected, confirmed, recurrent, complicated, and resolved disease states.

sections.heading: Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Acute, subacute, chronic, recurrent, or resolved state.
- Mild, moderate, severe, complicated, or life-threatening disease.
- Primary disease versus secondary complication or medication effect.
- Outpatient, emergency department, inpatient, ICU, pregnancy, pediatric, or older-adult setting.

sections.heading: Epidemiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The epidemiology of Mixed connective tissue disease depends on age, sex, geography, exposure, comorbid disease, medication use, pregnancy status when relevant, and ascertainment setting.
- In rheumatology, exam questions usually test the common population plus the high-risk exception.
- Risk estimates should be updated from local data or current guideline sources before publication.

sections.heading: Anatomy
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Identify the organ, tissue, vessel, nerve, compartment, joint, pathway, or system most directly involved in Mixed connective tissue disease.
- Map nearby structures that explain referred pain, spread, obstruction, ischemia, neurologic deficits, imaging findings, or procedure risk.
- For non-anatomic topics, focus on the structure most likely to change symptoms, testing, or treatment.

sections.heading: Physiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Define the normal function that Mixed connective tissue disease disrupts or depends on.
- Connect the physiology to symptoms, examination findings, laboratory abnormalities, imaging appearance, and treatment targets.
- Consider compensation, reserve, feedback loops, and decompensation triggers.

sections.heading: Pathophysiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- The central mechanism in Mixed connective tissue disease should explain why the patient develops the key symptoms and complications.
- Mechanisms may include obstruction, inflammation, ischemia, infection, immune injury, degeneration, trauma, thrombosis, endocrine imbalance, toxicity, neoplasia, or functional dysregulation.
- Distinguish the primary process from secondary complications and treatment-related harm.

sections.heading: Etiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider the common causes of Mixed connective tissue disease first, then actively check for dangerous but less common causes.
- Review congenital, acquired, infectious, inflammatory, traumatic, metabolic, neoplastic, medication-related, toxic, pregnancy-related, immune, and idiopathic causes when relevant.
- Use the time course and exposure history to narrow the cause.

sections.heading: Risk Factors
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Risk changes with age, family history, baseline disease, immune status, pregnancy, medications, procedures, travel, occupation, environment, and prior episodes.
- For Mixed connective tissue disease, risk factors should guide pretest probability and prevention, not replace objective assessment.
- Document modifiable risks that can be addressed during treatment or follow-up.

sections.heading: Genetics
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider inherited disease when presentation is early, familial, recurrent, syndromic, unusually severe, or associated with congenital findings.
- Ask about family history, ancestry when clinically relevant, consanguinity, pregnancy losses, and similar symptoms in relatives.
- Use genetic testing when results change diagnosis, screening, treatment, reproductive counseling, or family risk assessment.

sections.heading: Microbiology
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Consider infection when fever, exposure, immunocompromise, procedure, device, wound, systemic inflammation, or rapid clinical decline is present.
- Identify the likely source, route of spread, organism group, need for cultures, need for isolation, and need for source control.
- Antimicrobial decisions should account for severity, host risk, allergies, renal or hepatic function, pregnancy status, local resistance, and prior cultures.

sections.heading: Clinical Presentation
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Symptoms and signs depend on acuity, organ system involved, severity, comorbidities, medications, and prior episodes.
- Classic findings help recognition but absence of a classic finding does not exclude serious disease.
- Complications may be the first presentation in older adults, immunocompromised patients, and patients with limited follow-up.

sections.heading: History
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask onset, duration, progression, triggers, exposures, associated symptoms, prior episodes, and treatments already tried.
- Review medications, allergies, procedures, devices, travel, occupation, sexual history when relevant, pregnancy status when relevant, family history, and baseline function.
- For Mixed connective tissue disease, ask targeted questions that separate uncomplicated disease from dangerous complications.

sections.heading: Physical Examination
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Start with vital signs, mental status, perfusion, respiratory effort, hydration, pain severity, and overall appearance.
- Perform a focused rheumatology examination that directly tests the suspected mechanism.
- Look for systemic involvement, complications, mimics, and findings that change urgency or disposition.

sections.heading: Red Flags
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Shock, altered mental status, respiratory distress, severe hypoxemia, syncope, seizure, major bleeding, or rapidly progressive symptoms.
- Severe pain out of proportion, new neurologic deficit, threatened airway, sepsis, peritonitis, limb ischemia, compartment syndrome, or vision threat.
- Any Mixed connective tissue disease presentation with organ dysfunction, pregnancy risk, immunocompromise, failed initial treatment, or diagnostic uncertainty needs a lower threshold for escalation.

sections.heading: Differential Diagnosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Compare Mixed connective tissue disease with disorders that share the same dominant symptom, anatomic site, exposure, laboratory pattern, or imaging abnormality.
- Prioritize dangerous mimics that would be harmed by delay or by the wrong treatment.
- Use acuity, location, risk factors, examination findings, labs, imaging, and response to treatment to narrow the differential.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Approach
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- First decide whether Mixed connective tissue disease is unstable, complicated, recurrent, incidental, or safe for outpatient workup.
- Use the highest-yield initial test for the suspected syndrome rather than ordering broad panels without a decision point.
- If early tests are negative but suspicion remains high, reassess the history, repeat focused examination, and choose second-line testing based on the remaining differential.

sections.heading: Investigations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Use labs to assess severity, complications, treatment safety, and alternative diagnoses.
- Use imaging, bedside tests, cultures, tissue sampling, functional testing, or procedures when results change management.
- Interpret results with pretest probability and clinical context.
- Avoid tests that add risk, delay, or incidental findings without changing the next step.

sections.heading: Diagnostic Criteria
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Diagnosis of Mixed connective tissue disease should combine compatible clinical pattern with accepted laboratory, imaging, procedural, histologic, physiologic, or scoring criteria when available.
- Do not diagnose from one nonspecific abnormality when the full syndrome does not fit.
- Document uncertainty and the follow-up plan when the diagnosis remains provisional.

sections.heading: Severity Classification
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Severity depends on stability, organ dysfunction, symptom burden, anatomic extent, complications, treatment risk, and ability to follow up.
- Identify who needs urgent referral, admission, ICU care, procedure, surgery, isolation, rehabilitation, or close outpatient review.
- Use validated disease-specific scoring or staging systems when they exist and are available locally.

sections.heading: Management
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Assess stability first, then treat the underlying cause and the complications already present.
- Use supportive care, medications, procedures, rehabilitation, and prevention according to severity and contraindications.
- Escalate to specialty care when diagnosis is uncertain, severe disease is present, or treatment risk is high.

### Initial Management

- Confirm whether Mixed connective tissue disease is stable, unstable, incidental, recurrent, or complicated.
- Assess airway, breathing, circulation, mental status, pain, hydration, perfusion, and immediate threats.
- Escalate early when red flags, organ dysfunction, procedural need, or diagnostic uncertainty is present.

### Non-pharmacological Management

- Use education, observation, rehabilitation, isolation, procedural planning, or environmental change when it addresses rheumatology diagnosis, management, and prevention.
- Tailor activity, diet, wound, exposure, work, driving, return-to-play, or safety advice to the clinical setting.

### Pharmacological Management

- Use medication only when the indication is clear and contraindications have been checked.
- Verify dose, route, duration, interactions, allergies, renal and hepatic adjustment, pregnancy or lactation status, and monitoring.

### Procedures

- Use procedures when they confirm diagnosis, obtain tissue or fluid, provide source control, relieve obstruction, restore alignment, repair anatomy, or treat complications.
- Check consent, anatomy, imaging, bleeding risk, infection risk, sedation risk, equipment, and aftercare before proceeding.

### Surgical Management

- Surgical or procedural referral is needed for instability, failed medical management, obstruction, perforation, ischemia, uncontrolled bleeding, malignancy concern, repairable structural disease, or unsafe bedside management.
- Timing depends on severity, anatomy, operative risk, patient goals, and local expertise.

sections.heading: Monitoring
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Track symptoms, vital signs, function, pain, intake/output when relevant, focused examination findings, key labs, adverse effects, and response to therapy.
- For Mixed connective tissue disease, monitoring should detect deterioration, treatment toxicity, recurrence, and missed complications.
- Escalate if expected improvement does not occur or new red flags appear.

sections.heading: Follow-up
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Confirm diagnosis, severity, treatment response, adherence, side effects, and patient understanding.
- Arrange repeat examination, labs, imaging, rehabilitation, surveillance, medication review, or specialty care when indicated.
- Give a clear reassessment interval and explicit return precautions.

sections.heading: Complications
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Complications of Mixed connective tissue disease may be acute, chronic, recurrent, treatment-related, functional, psychosocial, or procedure-related.
- Prioritize complications that threaten life, airway, limb, vision, fertility, neurologic function, organ function, or public health.
- Document warning symptoms that require urgent reassessment.

sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Prognosis depends on cause, severity, timing of recognition, comorbid disease, age, treatment response, complications, and follow-up reliability.
- Early recognition of dangerous Mixed connective tissue disease patterns improves disposition and reduces preventable complications.
- Published prognostic statistics should be checked against current specialty sources before release.

sections.heading: Prevention
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Address modifiable risk factors, medication safety, vaccination or prophylaxis when relevant, exposure reduction, screening, and recurrence prevention.
- For Mixed connective tissue disease, prevention should target the cause, the complication pathway, and the follow-up failure points.
- Use patient-specific counseling rather than generic reassurance.

sections.heading: Patient Education
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Explain Mixed connective tissue disease in plain language, including what is known, what remains uncertain, and what symptoms should trigger urgent care.
- Review medication purpose, expected benefit, side effects, duration, and what to do if symptoms worsen.
- Confirm the patient understands follow-up, restrictions, wound or device care when relevant, and return precautions.

sections.heading: Nursing Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Monitor vital signs, pain, function, mental status, intake/output when relevant, wounds or devices, and response to treatment.
- Escalate fever, bleeding, respiratory change, neurologic change, perfusion change, uncontrolled pain, medication reaction, or worsening function.
- Reinforce education, discharge instructions, safe mobility, adherence, and follow-up logistics.

sections.heading: Pharmacy Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Check allergies, renal and hepatic function, pregnancy or lactation status, interactions, duplication, contraindications, adherence barriers, and monitoring needs.
- For Mixed connective tissue disease, medication review should include both treatment drugs and drugs that may cause, worsen, or mimic the problem.
- High-risk drugs require a documented indication, duration, monitoring plan, and counseling.

sections.heading: Dentistry Considerations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Ask about bleeding risk, anticoagulants, antiplatelets, immunosuppression, prosthetic material, active infection, and relevant medication adverse effects before invasive dental work.
- Coordinate with the treating clinician when medication interruption, prophylaxis, or infection control is being considered.
- Mixed connective tissue disease does not automatically imply dental antibiotic prophylaxis; use condition-specific indications and local guidance.

sections.heading: Special Populations
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Pediatrics: use age-specific normal values, developmental context, weight-based dosing, caregiver education, and safeguarding awareness.
- Pregnancy: stabilize the mother first, choose pregnancy-aware testing and medications when possible, and involve obstetrics for significant disease.
- Older adults: consider frailty, atypical presentation, falls, delirium, kidney function, polypharmacy, cognition, and goals of care.
- Renal or hepatic impairment: adjust diagnostic contrast, medications, dosing, and monitoring.
- Immunocompromised patients: lower the threshold for atypical infection, severe disease, cultures, imaging, admission, and interaction review.

sections.heading: Guideline Comparison
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current guidance for Mixed connective tissue disease may vary by country, specialty society, age group, pregnancy status, disease severity, and available resources.
- Compare local policy with major society guidance for diagnostic thresholds, treatment sequencing, drug choice, procedural timing, and follow-up intervals.
- Document where local practice differs from international guidance and why.

sections.heading: Clinical Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Stabilize first when the patient is unstable; diagnostic precision comes after immediate threats are controlled.
- Match each test to a decision it will change.
- Reassess after treatment because nonresponse is often a diagnostic clue.
- For Mixed connective tissue disease, the highest-yield clinical move is to identify severity and the next safe action.

sections.heading: Common Mistakes
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Treating Mixed connective tissue disease as a label instead of confirming mechanism, severity, and complications.
- Ordering broad tests without a focused question.
- Missing red flags because the initial symptom sounds common.
- Ignoring pregnancy status, renal function, hepatic function, allergies, interactions, or follow-up barriers.
- Discharging without clear return precautions.

sections.heading: Exam Pearls
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Step 1: know the mechanism, anatomy, microbiology, pharmacology, or physiology behind the topic.
- Step 2 CK: know the next best diagnostic or management step.
- Step 3: know disposition, monitoring, complications, follow-up, and patient safety.
- Board exams: know contraindications, high-risk exceptions, and when to escalate.

sections.heading: High-Yield Facts
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Know the classic presentation, first diagnostic step, first treatment step, dangerous complication, and follow-up need.
- Severity and stability decide disposition.
- Treatment should include prevention of recurrence when possible.

sections.heading: Evidence Gaps
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- This Mixed connective tissue disease draft still needs specialty review before publication.
- Medication doses, procedural thresholds, screening intervals, risk scores, and guideline claims should be localized.
- Imported notes, when present, should be reconciled with current evidence and cited sources.

sections.heading: References
sections.bodyMarkdown:
- Current specialty society guidance, local protocols, and institutional pathways should be checked during clinical review.
- When imported source notes are present, reconcile them against current references before publication.

### Source Excerpt: Mixed Connective Tissue Disease

_Source lines: 22509-22987._

```text
Mixed Connective Tissue Disease
- Mixed connective tissue disease is an "overlap" syndrome with clinical
features similar to those of SLE, RA, systemic sclerosis, and
polymyositis. Findings consistent with each of these diseases do not
necessarily occur simultaneously. It usually takes some time for a
pattern to be identified and a diagnosis of mixed connective tissue
disease to be made.

- Clinical findings include pulmonary involvement, esophageal
dysfunction, polyarthritis, sclerodactyly, cutaneous manifestations,
myopathy, and Raynaud phenomenon.

- The presence of anti-U1-RNP Abs is a key laboratory finding. High
ANA and RF may be present.

- Treatment varies according to which specific disease predominates.

Crystal-Induced Arthritides

Gout
A. General Characteristics
1. Gout is an inflammatory monoarticular arthritis caused by the
crystallization of monosodium urate in joints (see Table 6-6).

Hyperuricemia is a hallmark of the disease, but it does not by itself
indicate gout
2. Ninety percent of patients are men over 30 years of age. Women
are not affected until after menopause due to decreased estrogen-
mediated urinary urate excretion

## 3. Pathogenesis

a. Increased production of uric acid
- Hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase deficiency
-for example, in Lesch-Nyhan syndrome
- Phosphoribosyl pyrophosphate synthetase overactivity
d. Increased cell turnover associated with several conditions,
including cancer chemotherapy, chronic hemolysis, and
hematologic malignancies. Decreased excretion of uric acid
(accounts for 90% of cases)
- Renal disease
- NSAIDs, diuretics
- Acidosis
4. Pathophysiology of inflammation
a. PMNs play a key role in the acute inflammation of gout.

b. It develops when uric acid crystals collect in the synovial fluid
as the extracellular fluid becomes saturated with uric acid.

c. IgGs coat monosodium urate crystals, which are phagocytized
by PMNs, leading to the release of inflammatory mediators and
proteolytic enzymes from the PMNs, which then result in
inflammation.

B. Clinical Features
1. Asymptomatic hyperuricemia
a. Increased serum uric acid level in the absence of clinical
findings of gout, may be present without symptoms for 10 to 20
years or longer.

b. Should not be treated because over 95% of patients remain
asymptomatic.

TABLE 6-6 Major Arthritides
Osteoarthritis 	Rheumatoid
Arthritis
Gouty Arthritis

Onset 	Insidious 	Insidious 	Sudden
Common
locations
Weight-bearing
joints (knees,
hips, lumbar/
cervical spine),
hands
Hands (PIP, MCP),
wrists, ankles, knees
Great toe, ankles,
knees, elbows
Presence of
inflammation
No 	Yes 	Yes
Radiographic
changes
Narrowed joint
space,
osteophytes,
subchondral
sclerosis,
subchondral cysts
Narrowed joint
space, bony
erosions
Punched-out
erosions with
overhanging rim of
cortical bone
Laboratory
findings
None 	Elevated ESR, RF,
anemia
Crystals
Other
features
- No systemic
findings
- Bouchard
nodes and
Heberden
nodes in
hands
- Systemic
findings-extra-
articular
manifestations
common
- Ulnar deviation,
swan-neck, and
boutonniere
deformity
- Tophi
- Nephrolithiasis
1. Acute gout flare
a. Peak age of onset is 40 to 60 years of age for men.

b. Initial attack usually involves sudden onset of exquisite pain.

Pain often wakes the patient from sleep.

- Most often affects the big toe-the first metatarsophalangeal
joint (podagra). Other common joints affected are ankles
and knees.

d. Pain, erythema, swelling, tenderness, and warmth.

e. Fever may or may not be present.

f. As it resolves, the patient may have desquamation of overlying
skin.

Quick HIT
Precipitants of an Acute Gouty Attack

- Diet (e.g., fatty foods, alcohol)
- Dehydration
- Trauma
- Medications (e.g., diuretics)
1. Intercritical gout
a. An asymptomatic period after the initial attack, which is
unusual after other rheumatologic disorders and thus suggests
the diagnosis of gout.

b. Sixty percent of patients have a recurrence within 1 year. Some
patients (fewer than 10%) never have another attack of gout.

c. There is a 75% likelihood of a second attack within the first 2
years.

d. Attacks tend to become polyarticular with increased severity
over time.

2. Chronic tophaceous gout
a. Occurs in people who have had poorly controlled gout for more
than 10 to 20 years
b. Tophi
- Aggregations of urate crystals surrounded by giant cells in
an inflammatory reaction.

- Tophi cause deformity and destruction of hard and soft
tissues. In joints, they lead to destruction of cartilage and
bone, triggering secondary degeneration and development of
arthritis. They may be extra-articular.

e. Common locations of tophi: Extensor surface of forearms,
elbows, knees, Achilles tendons, and pinna of external ear
Quick HIT
Do a Gram stain and culture of the synovial fluid to rule out septic
arthritis, which is the most worrisome diagnosis on the differential
list.

C. Diagnosis
1. Joint aspiration and synovial fluid analysis (under a polarizing
microscope) is the only way to make a definitive diagnosis-
needle-shaped and negatively birefringent urate crystals
appear in synovial fluid.

2. Serum uric acid is not helpful in diagnosis because it can be normal
even during an acute gouty attack.

3. Radiographs reveal punched-out erosions with an overhanging rim
of cortical bone in advanced disease.

Quick HIT
Complications of Gout
- Nephrolithiasis-risk is small (less than 1% per year)
- Degenerative arthritis occurs in less than 15% of patients.

D. Treatment
1. In all stages, avoid secondary causes of hyperuricemia
a. Medications that increase uric acid levels (thiazide and loop
diuretics)
b. Obesity
c. Reduce alcohol intake
d. Reduce dietary purine intake, limit intake of seafood/red meat
Quick HIT
In acute gout, avoid aspirin (can aggravate the problem),
acetaminophen (has no anti-inflammatory properties), and urate-
lowering drugs (initiation during flare may worsen or prolong the
flare). Initiate urate-lowering pharmacotherapy once the acute flare
has resolved.

1. Acute gout
a. Rest the affected joint
b. Treatment is based on many factors, including comorbidities,
and on what treatments have worked best for the patient in the
past. NSAIDs, colchicine, and steroids are all acceptable first-
line treatments
c. NSAIDs
- One of the first-line treatments for an acute gout flare
(indomethacin is traditionally used, but other NSAIDs are
effective)
- Avoid in patients with history of peptic ulcer disease and GI
bleed, acute or chronic kidney disease, heart failure, or
cirrhosis
f. Colchicine
- An alternative for patients who cannot take NSAIDs or did
not respond to NSAIDs.

- High-dose treatment leads to significant GI side effects, thus
lower-dose regimens are favored
- Dose must be reduced in renal insuficiency and can cause
cytopenia

j. Corticosteroids
- Oral prednisone (7- to 10-day taper typically)
- Intra-articular corticosteroid injections (if only one joint is
involved)-dramatic relief of symptoms
- Avoid system steroids if concern for active infection, brittle
diabetes
2. Chronic treatment
a. Wait until patient has had at least two acute gouty attacks
before initiating prophylactic therapy. This is because the
second attack may take years to occur (if at all), and so the risk-
to-benefit ratio for prophylactic medication is not favorable
after one gouty attack. The presence of tophi is also an
indication for prophylactic therapy.

b. When giving prophylaxis, add either colchicine or an NSAID for
3 to 6 months to prevent an acute attack. The colchicine or
NSAID can then be discontinued, and the patient can remain on
the uricosuric agent or allopurinol indefinitely.

c. First-line therapy is typically allopurinol, though high-risk
groups should be tested for HLA-B*5801 first (risk of severe
hypersensitivity reactions including Stevens-Johnson syndrome
and TEN). Allopurinol is a xanthine oxidase inhibitor, which
decreases uric acid synthesis.

- Uricosuric drugs (probenecid, lesinurad, sulfinpyrazone) are
added if the uric acid goal is not met and if urinary urate
excretion is low or normal.

- For patients who are HLA-B*5801 positive, or cannot
achieve goal uric acid levels on allopurinol, the xanthine
oxidase inhibitor febuxostat is used.

- Uricases (pegloticase, rasburicase)-These drugs catalyze
the conversion of urate into allantoin, a more soluble
purine degradation product. They cause rapid lowering of
serum urate levels.

Pseudogout (Calcium Pyrophosphate
Deposition Disease)
A. General Characteristics
1. Calcium pyrophosphate crystals deposit in joints, leading to
inflammation.

2. Risk factors
a. Deposition increases with age and with OA of the joints.

Therefore, pseudogout is common in elderly patients with
degenerative joint disease.

b. Other conditions that may increase crystal deposition include
hemochromatosis, hyperparathyroidism, hypothyroidism,
and Bartter syndrome.

Quick HIT
Presentation of pseudogout is similar to gout, but typically occurs in
larger joints (knee).

B. Clinical Features
1. The most common joints affected are knees and wrists.

2. It is classically monoarticular, but can be polyarticular.

C. Diagnosis
1. Joint aspirate is required for definitive diagnosis-weakly
positively birefringent, rod-shaped and rhomboidal crystals in
synovial fluid (calcium pyrophosphate crystals) (Figure 6-7)
2. Radiographs-chondrocalcinosis (cartilage calcification)
D. Treatment
1. Treat the underlying disorder (if identified)
2. Symptomatic management is similar to that for gout
- First-line therapy includes NSAIDs
- Colchicine is useful for prophylaxis
- Intra-articular steroid injections, particularly with triamcinolone
6. Total joint replacement is appropriate if symptoms are debilitating
A 61-year-old man with a history of alcohol use disorder and parathyroid
adenoma presents with pain in his left knee for the last 2 days. He denies
fevers, chills, night sweats, or any history of trauma to the knee, but does
endorse abdominal pain and constipation. Physical examination is
significant for tenderness and erythematous skin overlying the left knee.

There is also marked swelling of the left knee. Laboratory findings reveal
an elevated calcium level (12.1 mg/dL).

If joint aspiration is performed, which of the following will be seen on
synovial fluid analysis?

A. Negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals
B. Positively birefringent rhomboid-shaped crystals
C. Neutrophil predominance with gram-positive cocci
D. Normal synovial fluid findings
- The answer is B: Positively birefringent rhomboid-shaped
crystals. This patient has a history of parathyroid adenoma that is

likely causing hypercalcemia secondary to hyperparathyroidism.

Patients with hypercalcemia are at risk for developing pseudogout, a
rheumatologic disease with diverse symptoms and signs arising from
the accumulation of calcium pyrophosphate dihydrate crystals in the
connective tissues. It commonly presents with acute onset, painful
monoarthropathy of the knee. Joint aspiration with synovial fluid
analysis confirms the diagnosis, showing rhomboid-shaped crystals
that are positively birefringent. (A) Negatively birefringent needle-
shaped crystals describe gout, which also presents with acute onset
monoarthropathy, but usually affects the first metatarsophalangeal
joint of the foot. Furthermore, gout is not triggered by
hypercalcemia. (C) Neutrophil predominant synovial fluid with
gram-positive cocci is diagnostic of septic arthritis. This patient has
no fever, making septic arthritis highly unlikely from the clinical
picture alone. (D) Normal synovial fluid findings are not the norm in
the setting of pseudogout.

FIGURE
6-7 Evaluation of joint pain.

Myopathies and Pain Syndromes
Idiopathic Inflammatory Myopathies
A. General Characteristics

## 1. Classification

a. Polymyositis (does not involve skin)
b. Dermatomyositis (associated with characteristic skin rash)
B. Causes
1. Pathologic inflammatory changes in muscle
a. Disease subtypes include dermatomyositis, polymyositis,
inclusion body myositis, antisynthetase syndrome, overlap
syndromes, and others.

C. Clinical Features
1. Features common to both polymyositis and dermatomyositis (see
Clinical Pearl 6-4)
a. Symmetrical proximal muscle weakness that develops
subacutely over weeks or several months
- The earliest and most severely affected muscle groups are
the neck flexors, shoulder girdle, and pelvic girdle muscles
- Distal extremity weakness is less frequent and typically less
severe
d. Myalgia in 33% of patients
e. Dysphagia in up to 30% of patients (involvement of striated
muscle in the upper GI tract)
f. More common in female patients
g. Associated findings include CHF, conduction defects,
arthralgias, and interstitial lung disease
2. Features unique to dermatomyositis
a. Heliotrope rash (butterfly)-around eyes, bridge of nose, cheeks
b. Gottron papules-papular, erythematous, scaly lesions over the
knuckles (MCP, PIP, DIP)
c. V sign-rash on the face, neck, and anterior chest
d. Shawl sign-rash on shoulders and upper back, elbows, and
knees
e. Periungual erythema with telangiectases
f. Subcutaneous calcifications in children-can be extremely
painful
g. Associated with vasculitis of the GI tract, kidneys, lungs, and

eyes (more common in children)
h. There is an increased incidence of malignancy in older adults
(lung, breast, ovary, GI tract, and myeloproliferative disorders).

Once dermatomyositis is diagnosed, make an effort to uncover
an occult malignancy. Dermatomyositis associated with
malignancy often remits once the tumor is removed.

CLINICAL PEARL 6-4
Diagnostic Criteria for Polymyositis
If two of first four -> possible polymyositis
If three of first four -> probable polymyositis
If all four -> definite polymyositis
- Symmetric proximal muscle weakness
- Elevation in serum creatine phosphokinase
- EMG findings of a myopathy
- Biopsy evidence of myositis
- Characteristic rash of dermatomyositis
D. Diagnosis

## 1. Laboratory

a. CK level is significantly elevated. CK levels correspond to the
degree of muscle necrosis, so one can monitor the disease
severity
b. LDH, aldolase, AST, ALT are also elevated
c. ANA in over 50%
d. Antisynthetase antibodies (anti-Jo-1 antibodies)-abrupt onset
of fever, cracked hands, Raynaud phenomenon, interstitial lung
disease and fibrosis, arthritis; does not respond well to therapy
e. Antisignal recognition particle
- Cardiac manifestations (common)
- Worst prognosis of all subsets
h. Anti-Mi-2 antibodies-better prognosis

## 2. EMG-abnormal in 90% of patients

3. Muscle biopsy
a. Shows inflammation and muscle fiber fibrosis
b. Dermatomyositis-perivascular and perimysial
c. Polymyositis and inclusion body myositis-endomysial
E. Treatment

1. Corticosteroids are the initial treatment
2. Steroid-sparing immunosuppressive agents (to reduce steroid-
induced side effects)-methotrexate, azathioprine
3. Physical therapy
Inclusion Body Myositis
A. General Characteristics
- More common in men (elderly)
- Insidious onset of slowly progressive proximal and distal weakness,
often leads to delay in diagnosis
- There is early weakness and atrophy of quadriceps, forearm flexors,
and tibialis anterior muscles. Involvement is asymmetrical. Facial
weakness occurs in one-third of patients, and dysphagia in one-half of
patients.

- Patients can also have loss of deep tendon reflexes (nerves are not
involved in polymyositis and dermatomyositis)
- Extramuscular manifestations are rare
- Diagnosis-slight elevation of CK levels (relatively low)
- Poor response to therapy
- Not associated with autoantibodies
Quick HIT
Inclusion body myositis is unique for the following reasons: Affects
male patients more than female patients, absence of
autoantibodies, distal muscle involvement, and relatively low
creatine kinase (CK); prognosis is poor.
```
